question.text: A regular premium life insurance policy with a 25-year term lapses after the policyholder stops paying premiums in the 2nd year. Which of the following is true regarding the policy's cash/surrender value?

answer.text: The policy does not acquire any cash or surrender value because premiums have been paid for less than 3 years
The policy acquires a minimum surrender value of 30% of premiums paid after 2 years
The policy acquires a surrender value after just 1 year of premium payment
The policyholder can claim the full refund of premiums paid within the first 2 years

explanation.text: **The Logic:** For life insurance policies with a term of 10 years or more, the policy acquires a cash/surrender value only after premiums have been paid for at least 3 consecutive years. Since this policyholder paid for only 2 years, the policy has lapsed without acquiring any surrender value. **The Trap:** The 2-year threshold for acquiring surrender value applies only to policies with a term of less than 10 years. For policies with a term of 10 years or more (like this 25-year policy), the threshold is 3 years. This distinction is a common source of confusion. **Cross-Exam Context:** Policy lapse and surrender value acquisition rules are regulatory knowledge tested in NISM X-B and IRDAI agent exams. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always emphasize to clients that the first 3 years of premium payment are critical -- stopping before that means losing everything. This is the most important window for premium discipline.
